caveats: a: No six-digit occupational distribution or direct task-exposure study was found.
a: The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may be distorted by R&D-heavy establishments or industry classification boundaries.
rho: Direct implementation evidence for independent LMM plants is unavailable.
rho: Drug, API, botanical-drug, and supplement regimes impose different validation and oversight burdens.
e: The assigned 133-firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed EBITDA-band population.
e: FDA establishment records do not directly identify independent firms or external-service revenue.
s5: Owner-age and succession evidence specific to the industry was not found.
s5: Distressed closures, product divestitures, and license transfers must be excluded unless a going concern changes control.
q: No representative contract dataset was available.
q: Retention differs substantially between proprietary ingredients, botanical extracts, commodity APIs, and toll production.
d5: CMS forecasts spending, not upstream constant-quality manufacturing volume.
d5: The NAICS basket includes medicinal and botanical products outside retail prescription drugs.
o: This estimates operator requirement for remaining demand, not the domestic share of supply.
o: Some low-complexity botanical products can be vertically integrated by customers.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat batch and product sales under supply, contract-manufacturing, private-label, or purchase-order relationships, commonly with quality specifications and customer qualification requirements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, finished-dose-only manufacturers classified elsewhere, distributors without manufacturing, research-only entities, shell registrants, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers of medicinal chemicals, active ingredients, botanical extracts, and related bulk preparations supplied repeatedly to external pharmaceutical, supplement, or formulation customers.

a.bridge: BLS data for other chemical-manufacturing groups show large embodied occupations such as equipment operators, packaging operators, mixers, supervisors, material movers, inspectors, and chemists rather than NAICS 325411 itself. FDA guidance simultaneously documents extensive review, testing, batch, supplier, quality, and manufacturing-control records. I infer AI exposure in the information-handling share while excluding laboratory execution, production, cleaning, maintenance, sampling, material movement, and accountable batch release; the proxy could understate scientific documentation in medicinal manufacturing and overstate similarity in plant automation.
caveats: No six-digit occupational distribution or direct task-exposure study was found.
The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and may be distorted by R&D-heavy establishments or industry classification boundaries.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from 15-25 LMM medicinal and botanical manufacturers, split among production, laboratory, quality, regulatory, supply-chain, sales, and administrative roles, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Documentation, deviation triage, literature and specification search, supplier review support, production planning, regulatory drafting, customer service, and commercial analysis are exposed. Physical extraction, synthesis, testing, packaging, sanitation, materials handling, and final quality decisions remain predominantly human or equipment work.

d5.bridge: CMS projects retail prescription-drug spending growth averaging 4.9% annually for 2026-2033, a broader nominal downstream market than medicinal chemicals and botanicals. I discount nominal spending for price and mix and assume only partial upstream transmission to derive modest positive constant-price demand; this may overstate the screened basket because CMS excludes many supplements yet includes expensive finished drugs whose value is not driven by ingredient volume.
caveats: CMS forecasts spending, not upstream constant-quality manufacturing volume.
The NAICS basket includes medicinal and botanical products outside retail prescription drugs.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price U.S. shipment volumes for NAICS 325411 by API, botanical extract, medicinal chemical, and supplement ingredient, plus five-year customer procurement forecasts, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Health demand, new products, supply-chain resilience, and repeat ingredient requirements support growth, while price pressure, overseas sourcing, product attrition, formulation efficiency, and customer concentration limit physical volume. FDA reports only 11% of API manufacturers are U.S.-based, creating both reshoring opportunity and strong import competition.

caveats: The assigned 133-firm count is margin-bridged rather than an observed EBITDA-band population.
FDA establishment records do not directly identify independent firms or external-service revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level reconciliation of SUSB establishments with FDA registration, ownership, product type, external-customer revenue, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Independent ingredient and extract manufacturers typically serve repeat external customers under specifications, fitting the lens. Captive plants, proprietary-product businesses without an outsourced-service character, research entities, distributors, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 325411 ($6,182,863,256) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 325411 ($15,480,254,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1801 (margins.json[325]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Chemical (Specialty) EBITDA/Sales 18.01%); revenue/employee $430,905 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 325411); class <5: 254 firms, avg revenue/firm $815,949 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $146,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,688,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $304,144 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,572,581 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $643,422 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 133 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,204,263 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,378,088 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 63 firms, avg revenue/firm $73,506,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,238,451 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 50 firms, avg revenue/firm $167,156,120 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,104,817 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 133

caveats: This estimates operator requirement for remaining demand, not the domestic share of supply.
Some low-complexity botanical products can be vertically integrated by customers.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing plans quantifying synthetic substitution, in-house production, continuous manufacturing, overseas sourcing, and elimination of current ingredients would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Physical medicinal chemicals and botanical extracts still require an accountable manufacturer to control raw materials, processes, testing, quality, and release. AI can assist records and analysis but cannot deliver physical product or assume legal quality responsibility; migration to foreign suppliers changes operator location rather than eliminating manufacturing.

caveats: No representative contract dataset was available.
Retention differs substantially between proprietary ingredients, botanical extracts, commodity APIs, and toll production.
q.change_evidence: A representative supply and contract-manufacturing agreement sample showing pricing basis, indexation, cost disclosure, productivity clauses, requalification costs, and renewal cadence would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Fixed product and batch prices allow savings to persist between negotiations, and qualification or validation costs can impede rapid supplier switching. Contract manufacturing rebids, open-book or cost-plus terms, generic ingredient competition, large-customer bargaining, and customer demands for continuous improvement should share part of the benefit.

caveats: Direct implementation evidence for independent LMM plants is unavailable.
Drug, API, botanical-drug, and supplement regimes impose different validation and oversight burdens.
rho.change_evidence: Audited labor-hour and quality-event results from GMP medicinal or botanical plants deploying validated AI for batch-record review, deviations, regulatory support, planning, and customer quality responses would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Retrieval, drafting, planning, and exception prioritization can be implemented within five years, but validated systems, data integrity, customer quality agreements, confidentiality, change control, and human approval constrain labor removal. FDA requires quality personnel to review and approve specifications, supplier qualification, tests, deviations, samples, and batches in relevant supplement manufacturing.

caveats: Owner-age and succession evidence specific to the industry was not found.
Distressed closures, product divestitures, and license transfers must be excluded unless a going concern changes control.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, FDA establishment ownership changes, and customer-quality requalification events reconciled to the eligible population would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Qualified plants, process know-how, customer approvals, and supply relationships are transferable assets and can attract strategic buyers. No observed control-transfer denominator for eligible LMM medicinal and botanical firms was found, and product or facility transactions are not necessarily firm control transfers.

principal_driver: A high reported compensation base paired with substantial regulated documentation and repeat, physically operator-required production.
principal_weakness: Validation, quality accountability, and poor six-digit task evidence limit confidence that exposed work can become realized labor savings.
